recipes.title: Private helpdesk route gate
recipes.promise: Keep customer-private helpdesk turns on approved local routes unless a policy explicitly allows managed fallback.
recipes.failure_story: A support agent needs to summarize a ticket with account notes and internal customer IDs. The old gateway treated the public model name as a plain provider alias, so one prompt-size or operator setting mistake could move private context to a cloud route without a clear receipt trail.
recipes.old_behavior: Routing was implicit: if the selected provider changed, the user saw the same model name but the operator had to reconstruct why from logs.
recipes.wardwright_behavior: The route policy narrows candidates before provider selection, fails closed if every candidate is removed, and records the policy constraint beside the selected route.
recipes.composition: Dispatcher baseline over local and managed targets, with request-policy constraints layered on top.

recipes.title: Context-window dispatcher
recipes.promise: Use the smallest route that fits the prompt, then preserve larger routes as explicit fallbacks.
recipes.failure_story: Long-running coding sessions often cross a context threshold gradually. Without a visible dispatcher, a small local model can start failing mysteriously or an expensive long-context model can become the silent default.
recipes.old_behavior: The caller chose a model name and learned about context limits only after provider failure or cost drift.
recipes.wardwright_behavior: Wardwright estimates prompt size, skips too-small targets before dispatch, and records the selected target plus skipped-context reasons.
recipes.composition: Flat dispatcher over concrete upstream targets today; nested selector DAGs are the planned extension for composing dispatchers inside cascades or alloys.

recipes.title: Partial alloy context ladder
recipes.promise: Blend local and managed model strengths while the prompt fits both; keep the larger window alone after the local context drops out.
recipes.failure_story: Calciforge-style synthetic models are useful when a local model is fast and private but a managed model has a larger context or different reasoning style. Operators need to see when both participate and when the local constituent was skipped.
recipes.old_behavior: Alloy-like routing was hard to explain because the public model name hid which constituent actually handled a request.
recipes.wardwright_behavior: The route planner supports partial-context alloys over concrete targets and receipts expose selected, fallback, and skipped constituents.
recipes.composition: Implemented for concrete upstream targets; synthetic-model-to-synthetic-model DAG composition is planned with cycle detection.

recipes.title: Deprecated SDK stream retry
recipes.promise: Catch a prohibited SDK name before it reaches the user, abort the attempt, add a targeted reminder, and release the fixed retry.
recipes.failure_story: A coding assistant keeps recommending an old client constructor across chunk boundaries. A prompt-level rule wastes context on every turn, but a plain output filter either leaks the bad token or blocks a useful answer.
recipes.old_behavior: The first bad stream either reached the user or the whole request failed with no repair path.
recipes.wardwright_behavior: A bounded holdback window detects the split stream match, stops unreleased generation, retries once with a precise reminder, and records both attempts.
recipes.composition: TTSR-style stream guard with a retry route that can move from the local first attempt to a review-capable managed route.

recipes.title: Credential redaction ladder
recipes.promise: Rewrite account-like spans, count related secret matches in session history, and escalate later turns only when repetition makes the session risky.
recipes.failure_story: An assistant can safely redact one account identifier, but repeated related secret-shaped matches in nearby turns suggest the session has drifted into credential handling.
recipes.old_behavior: Every match looked the same: either redact everything forever or alert too early.
recipes.wardwright_behavior: The policy distinguishes a safe rewrite from a state transition, uses bounded session history, and changes future turn routing after the threshold is reached.
recipes.composition: State machine plus stream rewrite policy; future turns can bind the review state to a managed route.

recipes.title: Tool loop cost brake
recipes.promise: Detect repeated write-capable tool calls and make the next action reviewable before the agent burns budget or mutates state twice.
recipes.failure_story: A repo agent keeps calling the same issue or pull-request tool after a transient error. The individual calls look valid, but the sequence is wasting time and may repeat an external side effect.
recipes.old_behavior: Only the final failure was visible; the repeated tool sequence was buried in trace noise.
recipes.wardwright_behavior: Tool context records namespace, tool name, risk class, call id, and session history so policy can deny, reroute, or alert on the repeated sequence.
recipes.composition: Tool policy over session-scoped history; compatible with route policy for switching to a stronger planner after repeated failures.

recipes.title: Done, but missing artifact
recipes.promise: Block or alert when the model claims success but the required artifact evidence is missing.
recipes.failure_story: An agent says the report, patch, or export is complete even though the file, PR, database row, or tool receipt does not exist. The natural-language answer sounds confident, so the failure is easy to miss.
recipes.old_behavior: Downstream users saw a successful message and discovered the missing artifact later.
recipes.wardwright_behavior: The final-output policy checks claimed completion against receipt evidence and marks the run ambiguous unless the artifact is present.
recipes.composition: Output policy over final response plus receipt facts; can be paired with structured-output validation when the consumer expects JSON.

recipes.title: Structured output repair gate
recipes.promise: Treat provider JSON/schema support as helpful but not sufficient: validate the final contract Wardwright promised to the caller.
recipes.failure_story: Different providers expose different structured-output constraints, and a caller may accept one of several valid shapes. A single-provider schema mode does not always match the synthetic model contract.
recipes.old_behavior: The app delegated schema correctness to whichever provider happened to run this turn.
recipes.wardwright_behavior: Wardwright owns the outer contract, can retry with validation feedback, and can record which schema branch or semantic check failed.
recipes.composition: Structured-output policy can sit after dispatcher, cascade, or alloy routing because the caller sees one stable synthetic model contract.
